item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The old one looks better though. The new one doesn't fit the description. Mainly, shouldn't it be green?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just learned about Sedna not long ago from a Sam O'Nella video, so as I was reading this the slow realization of what this was, was very cool to experience. Well done!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article has been replaced with this image, which is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Recently became a member, and this article gets my first upvote. Well deserved. Non-mainstream mythologies do not nearly get as much attention as they deserve.

I first misread cosmEtologist as cosmOtologist. Fun realization of my misread when the hairdresser was introduced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember seeing this image in a Ripley's Believe It Or Not book!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saw this in Confinement and I came here to read it. +1
I noticed a few typos: It should be Yupik, not Yukpik, and Iñupiat, not Inupiat. Otherwise, looks great!
In the version of the myth I read, Sedna only lost her fingers, not her whole hands. Is this an alternate version?
Why does the article describe her as a "corpse" if she is animate and can speak? (also I'd think she would be designated SCP-1836-3 or something like that). If she is considered a corpse, this should probably be tagged with "cadaver".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey sorry I didn't see this. I honestly don't know of the North American First Nations conception of how the bodies of whales work in deep-water. My understanding (which may be wrong) is that they largely kept to coastal areas and within archipelagos. Feel free to DM me if you want help with your skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP really stuck with me even as a lurker. I'm working on a skip based on "whale falls," when a whale dies and its corpse drops into deep water. The abundance of energy and nutrition that the corpse provides dramatically increases biological activity in its radius. Do you know of any First Nations ideas that deal explicitly with the coprses of cetaceans in a deep water context? I'd love to link to your skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a book of american folklore as a kid with pictures of Sedna in it. It's a sad, stupid, tragic story.

Luck be with you, Sedna.

item.title: Congrats for Confinement!
item.content:encoded: I've always liked this SCP, and it's great for it to get some more exposure via the Confinement series. It's an animated series on youtube of rather good quality. I can't +1 this SCP again, but hopefully there'll be others coming in to do that now!

item.title: Confinement brought me here.
item.content:encoded: I love this one a lot now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This scp is great and all…

but damn, that iceberg is nice on the eyes.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this page missing a Serpent's Hand tag?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that the containment protocols include a cosmetologist. There are a number of things I like about this, but that is what I keep coming back to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could upvote it twice. All i know of Inuit mythology is from Terror, but I like religious/mythologic SCPs, and I love non-Abrahamic, non-Far Eastern and non-Mayincatec SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somehow missed this. But yeah, YWHH's magic red cow is a thing.

Fair enough argument, but there's a ton of real religions out there that can offer narrative angles that haven't been explored before, so I still stand by what noob!me had to say regarding a need for a greater volume of religious, non-Abrahamic skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Found two small mistakes:

Fullbrush: Right, is there anything the Foundation could to to improve your ability to control SCP-1836?

On of those "to"s should be a "do".

After ten minutes of preparation and burning of incense they enter SCP-1836

This sentence is missing a period.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a very neat idea. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy 100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, definite upvote here. Glad I checked the discussion, too. Made it so much more wonderful by a factor of five at the very least. Everything about this scp makes me happy, most of which has already been said and much better than I could, but not only is this an incredible non abrahamic religious scp it genuinely inspires me to maybe do something like this myself, and it also makes me happy that native north american culture is being recognized. Especially native cultures from what is now Canada, for some reason a lot of non native people don't really pay as much attention to native plights when they're not from what is now the US.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep. So did I! (I'd have signed in anyway, but… strong motivation here!)

This is very good.

I notice there are still various spellings for the shaman's name, but I really thought it was deliberate. Like Western people can't be bothered… which is a nice additional touch, and gives the article even more a note of 'we can't win' ("we" representing just about everyone involved).
I would have liked to know more about all the extinct species living inside the iceberg. Too bad we don't know exactly what they do in there (except nibble on people a bit when things get rough!?). And does this mean that SCP-1836 has been around for millions of years?

A skip that defends cetaceans from whale hunters. YAAAAYYYY!!!!

Hail Featured SCP! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Noting that the image is from this gallery: http://www.antarctica.gov.au/about-antarctica/antarctic-images/parliament-house-exhibitions/2006/icebergs

And is under this copyright: http://www.antarctica.gov.au/about-us/copyright

which states:

[…] You may download, store in cache, display, print and reproduce the material in unaltered form only (retaining this notice, or links to it where they appear) for your personal, non-commercial use or use within your organisation. […]

Links to both the image gallery and the copyright are now present in the image caption of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
YHWH's magic red cow

First off: Is that actually a thing?

Also, I'll partially disagree. While exploring religious aspects that are generally not explored in Amero-European contexts is always fun and can lead to vastly different approaches than Abrahamic based ones (on which there is a lot of competition, both on site and on centuries of very referred texts for those of us in either of those continents), in the end I don't think that origin, per se, is what makes religious skips better or worse. Don't get me wrong, many of these "non-mainstream" religions can contain worldviews which are so "foreign" to us that they make for wonderful narratives, but well developed fictional religions can also be great and even in-depth, for the scope of the narrative we generally do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Signed in just to upvote this, its quality exceeds my laziness

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, whenever people complain about Abrahamic SCPs, this is what they're saying we need more of. No Cthulhu F'tagn crap, give us stories about religious entities that are/were really worshipped/revered, are not connected to Jesus and Friends, and haven't been done before. As much as I like Broken God stuff, I'd rather see more Native American, pre-Christianity European, and aboriginal Australian SCPs than more stuff about WAN and his holy Wi-Fi signals or YWHY's magic red cow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I tried hard to do this myth justice. I was raised on a diet First Nation's oral tradition and I want/need more of it out there in the popular culture. I'm glad I made you happy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an Inupiaq, I really, really, REALLY loved this piece!

Within the walls of this chamber is a frozen, well-preserved, corpse of a young woman of Inupiat ethnicity. The corpse had suffered injuries in the past and is missing its hands at the wrist. Only the head of the corpse is exposed to the open air. Due to safety concerns only the on-site shaman and cosmetologist are to have access to this chamber.

Only the Foundation would upgrade Sedragina's shamanic groomer to a full cosmetologist. The blend of Inuit myth and clinical tone really made this skip for me. I mean, where I grew up I heard a different version, but such is mythology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly it was the research angle on this thing that made me the most happy. Looking into what the shamans would try to symbolically do to placate her… the various taboos of Inuit shamanism… Such fun. Such fun. I have another skip in the pipeline that deals with Sedna.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That I do. That I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
deal with long, lady's, hair.

Only problem I see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As briefly mentioned below, it's based on the mythology of Sedna. Sedna is the Inuit goddess of the sea and marine animals; her fingers became the animals, which is why she no longer has any. When she's angry (her children (the animals) being hunted improperly, her hair tangled, etc.) then there are storms. Her husband is named in the text, of course. They, er, don't get along, as you probably guessed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is much better with the context from the WL post, the absurdism doesn't translate without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So good news, this is a great entry, with the slightest hint of a possible "Serpent's Hand" involvement.

Bad news, if I don't write this comment it will be completely eclipsed by 2000.

My main criticism is that you have horrible timing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it -1 or -A?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made fixes blah blah blah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck yeah, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has been stewing in my head for a real long time. I'd like to thank Photosynthetic, Flameshirt and Gaffsey for helping me with edits!

This article was originally going to be posted a year ago but it got delayed due to my involvement in the WL. Incidentally this article has a WL counterpart Sedna V. Ataciara the Qaqulluk .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shaman's name issue has been feexed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really awesome and I'd love to know what it's based on. Check that collapsible and the interview for typos; I know if nothing else, the shaman's name isn't always spelled the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could feel the Serpent's Hand involvement in this from the beginning, for some reason. I can't say why, just, everything pointed to them being present. That's not a bad thing, this is a very good article. Though, I have to admit the idea of divorced Gods with restraining orders is absolutely hilarious. +1
